corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: summarization based on text extraction is inherently limited but generation-style abstractive methods have proven challenging to build in this work we propose a fully data-driven approach to abstractive sentence summarization our method utilizes a local attention-based model that generates each word of the summary conditioned on the input sentence while the model is structurally simple it can easily be trained end-to-end and scales to a large amount of training data the model shows significant performance gains on the duc-2004 shared task compared with several strong baselines body

top_candidates.sentence: The PREFIX baseline actually performs sur-prisingly well on ROUGE-1 which makes sense given the earlier observed overlap between article and summary.
Both ABS and MOSES+ perform better than TOPIARY, particularly on ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-L in DUC.

The full model ABS+ scores the best on these tasks, and is significantly better based on the default ROUGE confidence level than TOPIARY on all metrics, and MOSES+ on ROUGE-1 for DUC as well as ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L for Gigaword.
For these experiments we look at the perplexity of the system as a language model on validation data, which controls for the variable of inference and tuning.
Both the convolutional encoder and the attention-based encoder further reduce the perplexity, with attention giving a value below 30.
The abstractive nature of the system helps, but for ROUGE even using pure extractive generation is effective.

Model Encoder Perplexity KN-Smoothed 5-Gram none 183.2 Feed-Forward NNLM none 145.9 Bag-of-Word enc1 43.6 Convolutional (TDNN) enc2 35.9 Attention-Based (ABS) enc3 27.1 Table 2: Perplexity results on the Gigaword validation set comparing various language models with C=5 and endto-end summarization models.

R-1 R-2 R-L Greedy ABS+ Abs 26.67 6.72 21.70 Beam BOW Abs 22.15 4.60 18.23 Beam ABS+ Ext 27.89 7.56 22.84 Beam ABS+ Abs 28.48 8.91 23.97 Table 3: ROUGE scores on DUC-2003 development data for various versions of inference.
Figure gives a schematic representation of the decoder architecture.
Including even the bag-of-words encoder reduces perplexity number to below 50.
In this work, we propose a fully data-driven approach to abstractive sentence summarization.
This allows us to train a summarization model for headline-generation on a corpus of article pairs from Gigaword consisting of around 4 million articles.
Our approach outperforms a machine translation system trained on the same large-scale dataset and yields a large improvement over the highest scoring system in the DUC-2004 competition.
Our approach outperforms a machine translation system trained on the same large-scale dataset and yields a large improvement over the highest scoring system in the DUC-2004 competition.
Our approach outperforms a machine translation system trained on the same large-scale dataset and yields a large improvement over the highest scoring system in the DUC-2004 competition.

Contrast this to a fully extractive sentence summary which transfers words from the input: or to the related problem of sentence compression that concentrates on deleting words from the input: While abstractive summarization poses a more difficult generation challenge, the lack of hard constraints gives the system more freedom in generation and allows it to fit with a wider range of training data.

The next section defines a parameterization for this distribution, in Section 4, we return to the question of generation for factored models, and in Section 5 we introduce a modified factored scoring function.
We combine this probabilistic model with a generation algorithm which produces accurate abstractive summaries.
Summarization is an important challenge of natural language understanding.
Next we consider ablations to the model and algorithm structure.
